cards.heading: Fundamentals
cards.content: Learn core JavaScript concepts like variables, functions, and scope...

pageContent.description: JavaScript (JS) is a versatile, high-level programming language primarily used for web development. It enables dynamic content on websites, runs on the client side, and supports event-driven programming, enhancing user interactivity.
pageContent.para1: The console is a browser developer tool for debugging. Alert shows popup messages, while document.write() outputs to the HTML document (use sparingly).
pageContent.code3:
console.log("Hello, World!");
// Output: Hello, World! (in console)
pageContent.para4: Displays a popup dialog box
pageContent.code4:
alert("Hello, World!");
// Shows popup with message
pageContent.para5: document.write() is used when you want to print the content to the HTML document.
pageContent.code5:
document.write("

Hello, World!

"); // Writes to HTML page as heading

questions.text: Which method is primarily used for debugging in the browser console?
What does the alert() function do?
Why should document.write() be used sparingly?
options: It can overwrite page content if called after load
questions.correct_answer: It can overwrite page content if called after load

questions.text: Which of these displays 'Hello World' directly in the HTML document?
Is JavaScript primarily a client-side or server-side language?

exercises.title: Display Hello Mentor
exercises.description: Log 'Hello JS-Mentor' to the console and use alert() to show 'Welcome to the course'.

pageContent.description: Explore the fundamental concepts of JavaScript, including variables, data types, and basic syntax. Learn the building blocks that form the foundation of JavaScript programming.
pageContent.para1: Understand the concept of variables and explore various data types such as strings, numbers, and booleans. Learn how to declare and manipulate variables in JavaScript.
pageContent.para3: The if statement is a fundamental control flow structure in JavaScript.
pageContent.para4: Explore different types of loops: for loop, while loop, and do-while loop.
pageContent.para5: Learn the concept of functions, how to define them, and their role in code organization and reusability.

questions.text: Which keyword is used to declare a variable that can be changed?
What is the output of typeof 'John'?
Which statement is used for decision-making based on a condition?
What is the purpose of a for loop?
options: To repeat code a specific number of times
questions.correct_answer: To repeat code a specific number of times

questions.text: Why are functions important in programming?
options: To make the page load faster

exercises.title: Variable Checker
exercises.description: Create a variable 'age', check if it's over 18, and log 'Adult' or 'Minor' to the console.

pageContent.description: Setting up a proper development environment is essential for JavaScript programming. This guide covers the basic setup, tools, and service worker configuration.
pageContent.para1: To begin JavaScript development, you'll need: 1) A code editor (VS Code recommended), 2) A modern browser (Chrome/Firefox), and 3) Node.js for package management. These tools provide everything needed for writing, testing, and debugging JavaScript code.
pageContent.para2: Key tools for JavaScript development include:
pageContent.code2:
// Recommended VS Code extensions:
1. ESLint - Code linting
2. Prettier - Code formatting
3. Live Server - Local development server
4. Debugger for Chrome - Browser debugging

// Essential npm packages:
npm install -g nodemon eslint prettier
pageContent.para4: Service workers are scripts that run in the background, enabling offline functionality, push notifications, and background sync. They act as a proxy between web apps and the network.
pageContent.para5: Service workers must be registered from your main JavaScript file. They only work over HTTPS (except on localhost).
pageContent.para6: Chrome DevTools provides comprehensive service worker debugging tools under Application > Service Workers. Check for updates, inspect caches, and simulate offline conditions.

questions.text: Which tool is recommended as a code editor for JS development?
What is the purpose of Node.js in a development environment?
Where can you debug Service Workers in Chrome DevTools?
Service Workers primarily enable which functionality?
Service workers require which protocol to work (except on localhost)?

exercises.title: Service Worker Init
exercises.description: Write a script that checks if 'serviceWorker' is in 'navigator' and logs 'SW Supported' if true.

pageContent.description: JavaScript (JS) is a powerful scripting language widely used for web development. In this chapter, we'll embark on the journey of learning JavaScript from scratch.
pageContent.para1: Before diving into JavaScript, let's understand the basics of coding. Coding is the process of converting human-readable instructions into a language that computers can understand and execute.
pageContent.para2: Creating a conducive development environment is crucial. We'll guide you through setting up a code editor, version control, and other tools to make your coding experience smooth.
pageContent.para4: Variables are containers for storing data values. In JavaScript, you can declare variables using 'var', 'let', or 'const'.
pageContent.para5: JavaScript has various data types, including 'string', 'number', 'boolean', 'null', and 'undefined'. Each data type serves a unique purpose.
pageContent.para6: JavaScript is dynamically typed, meaning you don't have to declare the data type explicitly. The interpreter determines the type during execution.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a keyword for variable declaration?
What does 'dynamic typing' mean in JavaScript?
options: Data types are determined during execution
questions.correct_answer: Data types are determined during execution

questions.text: Which data type represents the intentional absence of any object value?
What is the difference between let and const?
What is the result of let x; console.log(x);?

exercises.title: Const vs Let
exercises.description: Declare a const variable for your 'birthYear' and a let variable for 'currentScore'. Log both to the console.

pageContent.description: JavaScript (JS) is a versatile, high-level programming language primarily used for web development. It enables dynamic content on websites, runs on the client side, and supports event-driven programming, enhancing user interactivity.
pageContent.para1: As we embark on the journey of JavaScript, let's delve into the fundamental concepts of declaring variables and constants. Open your preferred text editor and start exploring the following examples:
pageContent.para2: In JavaScript, variables serve as containers for storing data values. You can declare variables using 'var', 'let', or 'const'.
pageContent.para4: Variables declared outside any function or block have a global scope, allowing access throughout the entire program.
pageContent.para5: Variables declared within a function or block have a local scope, limiting access to that specific function or block.
pageContent.para6: Constants, defined with 'const', retain their values and cannot be reassigned. They offer a means to set values that remain constant.

questions.text: Which variable declaration is function-scoped rather than block-scoped?
What happens if you try to reassign a value to a const variable?
A variable declared outside any function has what kind of scope?
Which keyword is preferred for variables that should not be reassigned?
Local scope variables are accessible from where?

exercises.title: Global vs Local
exercises.description: Define a global variable outside a function. Inside the function, define a local variable with the same name. Log both.

pageContent.description: JavaScript (JS) is a versatile, high-level programming language primarily used for web development. It enables dynamic content on websites, runs on the client side, and supports event-driven programming, enhancing user interactivity.
pageContent.para1: Operators are symbols that perform operations on variables and values. JavaScript supports various types of operators, including arithmetic, assignment, comparison, logical, and more.
pageContent.para2: Expressions are combinations of values, variables, and operators that can be evaluated to a single value. They form the basis of JavaScript's dynamic behavior.
pageContent.code2:
// Example: Arithmetic Expression
let result = (x + y) * 2;
pageContent.para4: The simple assignment operator '=' assigns a value to a variable.
pageContent.code4:
// Example: Simple Assignment
let z = 15;
pageContent.para5: The addition assignment operator '+=' adds the right operand to the left operand and assigns the result to the left operand.
pageContent.para6: The increment assignment operator '++' adds 1 to the operand's current value.

questions.text: What is the result of 10 + 5 in JS?
Which operator is used for 'addition assignment'?
Which operator is used to find the remainder of a division?
What is an expression in JavaScript?
options: A combination that evaluates to a value
questions.correct_answer: A combination that evaluates to a value

exercises.title: Rectangle Area
exercises.description: Calculate the area of a rectangle with width 10 and height 20 using arithmetic operators. Print the result.

pageContent.description: Control flow statements determine the execution order of JavaScript code. Learn conditional logic and loops to control program behavior.
pageContent.para1: Conditional statements allow different code blocks to execute based on specified conditions. Master these fundamentals to make decisions in your code.
pageContent.para3: Execute code only when a condition is true:
pageContent.para4: Handle the alternative case when the condition is false:
pageContent.para5: Chain multiple conditions together:
pageContent.para6: Clean alternative for multiple conditions comparing the same value:
pageContent.para8: Repeat code a specific number of times:
pageContent.para10: Execute at least once, then check condition:

questions.text: When is the else block executed in an if/else statement?
options: When the if condition is false
When the if condition is true
questions.correct_answer: When the if condition is false

questions.text: Which statement is a clean alternative for multiple conditions?
What is the key difference between while and do-while loops?
Which keyword is used to exit a switch case?
What happens if you forget a break in a switch statement?
options: It falls through to the next case
questions.correct_answer: It falls through to the next case

exercises.title: Loop with Skip
exercises.description: Write a for loop that prints numbers 1 to 10, but use 'continue' to skip the number 5.

pageContent.description: JavaScript (JS) is a versatile, high-level programming language primarily used for web development. It enables dynamic content on websites, runs on the client side, and supports event-driven programming, enhancing user interactivity.
pageContent.para1: Since the early days of programming, it's been a tradition to write a 'Hello World' program as your first step. Let's continue that legacy. Open your favorite text editor and write the program below:
pageContent.code1:
// Hello World Program in JavaScript
console.log('Hello, World!');
pageContent.para2: In JavaScript, variables are containers for storing data values. You can declare variables using 'var', 'let', or 'const'. Each has different scoping rules and use cases.
pageContent.para4: Variables declared outside any function or block have a global scope, meaning they are accessible throughout the entire program.
pageContent.para5: Variables declared within a function or block have a local scope. They are only accessible within that specific function or block.
pageContent.para6: Constants are declared using 'const' and cannot be reassigned once a value is set. They are used to define values that should remain constant throughout the program.

questions.text: How do you call a function named myFunction?
What is the scope of a variable declared with let inside a function?
Can a global variable be accessed inside a function?
options: Only if passed as a prop

questions.text: Why use constants for values like PI?
What is the primary purpose of using functions?

exercises.title: Multiply Function
exercises.description: Create a function named 'multiply' that takes two parameters 'a' and 'b' and returns their product.

pageContent.description: JavaScript (JS) is a versatile, high-level programming language primarily used for web development. It enables dynamic content on websites, runs on the client side, and supports event-driven programming, enhancing user interactivity.
pageContent.para1: An array is a special variable in JavaScript that can hold more than one value at a time. Arrays are useful for storing lists of items, such as numbers, strings, or objects.
pageContent.para2: JavaScript provides many useful methods to work with arrays, such as push(), pop(), shift(), unshift(), and more. These methods help in adding, removing, or modifying elements.
pageContent.para4: Objects in JavaScript are collections of key-value pairs. They are used to store related data and functions together.
pageContent.para5: You can access object properties using dot notation or bracket notation.
pageContent.para6: JavaScript allows arrays and objects to be nested inside one another. This is useful for representing more complex data structures.

questions.text: How do you access the first element of an array named fruits?
Which array method adds an element to the end of the array?
What is an object in JavaScript?
Which notation is used to access an object property using a variable?
How can you store multiple people's data in a single variable?

exercises.title: Fruit Object
exercises.description: Create an array 'fruits' with 3 names. Then create an object 'car' with 'brand' and 'model' properties.

pageContent.description: JavaScript (JS) is a versatile, high-level programming language primarily used for web development. It enables dynamic content on websites, runs on the client side, and supports event-driven programming, enhancing user interactivity.
pageContent.para1: Debugging is the process of identifying and resolving errors or bugs in your code. In JavaScript, various tools and techniques are available to help developers troubleshoot and fix problems effectively.
pageContent.code1:
// Example: Simple Debugging
console.log('Debugging started');
pageContent.para2: JavaScript offers several ways to debug code, including logging to the console, using breakpoints, and stepping through code with developer tools.
pageContent.para3: Using 'console.log()' is one of the most common and effective ways to debug your code by printing variable values or messages to the browser console.
pageContent.para4: Breakpoints can be set in the browser's developer tools to pause code execution at specific lines, allowing you to inspect the current program state.
pageContent.para5: The debugger lets you execute your code one line at a time to better understand its behavior and locate issues.
pageContent.para7: The 'try-catch' statement is used to handle runtime errors gracefully. It lets you write code that may fail inside the 'try' block and catch errors in the 'catch' block.
pageContent.para8: The 'throw' statement is used to generate custom errors intentionally. These can be caught using 'try-catch' for better control over flow and messaging.

questions.text: Which block is used to catch errors?
What is the purpose of the finally block?
options: Only runs if an error occurs

questions.text: Which keyword is used to manually throw an error?
What does the try block do?
options: Wraps code that might cause an error
questions.correct_answer: Wraps code that might cause an error

questions.text: Which tool is built-in to browsers for JS debugging?

exercises.title: Safe JSON Parse
exercises.description: Write a try...catch block that attempts to parse a string '{"name": "Alice"}'. Log the name if successful.

pageContent.description: Closures and callbacks are fundamental concepts in JavaScript programming. Closures allow functions to retain access to variables from their outer scope even after the outer function has finished executing. Callbacks, on the other hand, are functions passed as arguments to another function and executed at a later point in time, often used for asynchronous operations.
pageContent.para1: A closure is created when a function is defined within another function, allowing the inner function to access the outer function's variables. This mechanism provides a way to create private variables and encapsulate functionality.
pageContent.para3: A callback function is a function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed later.
pageContent.para4: Callbacks are commonly used to handle asynchronous operations, ensuring that certain code executes only after the completion of an asynchronous task.
pageContent.para5: Callbacks are widely used in event handling, AJAX requests, and other scenarios where asynchronous behavior is essential.

exercises.title: The Counter Closure
exercises.description: Create a function 'makeCounter' that returns a function. Every time the returned function is called, it should increment and return a private variable.

options: A function that remembers outer variables
A way to close a browser tab
questions.correct_answer: A function that remembers outer variables

options: A function passed as an argument
questions.correct_answer: A function passed as an argument

questions.text: Can a closure access its parent's parent scope?

pageContent.description: Promises are a powerful abstraction for handling asynchronous operations in JavaScript. They provide a cleaner and more structured way to deal with asynchronous code compared to traditional callbacks. This chapter explores the concept of promises and their role in managing asynchronous tasks.
pageContent.para1: A promise represents the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation and its resulting value. It allows you to handle asynchronous code in a more synchronous and readable manner.
pageContent.para3: A promise can be in one of three states: pending, fulfilled, or rejected. The state transitions based on the completion or failure of the asynchronous operation.
pageContent.para4: Promises can be chained together, allowing for more structured and readable asynchronous code. This is achieved through the use of the `then` method.
pageContent.para5: The `catch` method is used to handle errors in promise chains. It captures any rejection that may occur during the execution of the asynchronous operation.

questions.text: What are the three states of a Promise?
Which method handles a resolved Promise?
Which method handles a rejected Promise?

options: Waits for all promises to resolve
questions.correct_answer: Waits for all promises to resolve

questions.text: Why is async logic important in JS?

exercises.title: Delayed Greeting
exercises.description: Write a function 'delayedGreeting' that takes a name and a callback. Use setTimeout to print 'Hello [name]' after 1 second, then run the callback.

pageContent.description: Event handling is a crucial aspect of web development, allowing you to respond to user interactions and create dynamic and interactive user interfaces. This chapter delves into the concepts of event handling, event listeners, and the various events triggered by user actions.
pageContent.para1: Events are occurrences that happen in the browser, such as user actions (clicks, keypresses, etc.) or system events. Event handling involves writing code to respond to these events and perform actions accordingly.
pageContent.para3: Event listeners are functions that wait for a specific event to occur and then execute a block of code in response. They are commonly used to handle user interactions.
pageContent.para4: Event propagation refers to the order in which events are triggered and handled. Understanding event propagation is essential for managing complex event interactions.
pageContent.para5: The event object contains information about the event, such as the type of event, the target element, and additional properties. It is often passed to event handler functions.

options: A function that waits for user actions
A tool to listen to music
questions.correct_answer: A function that waits for user actions

questions.text: Which method adds an event listener?

options: Events travel from child to parent
Events travel from parent to child
questions.correct_answer: Events travel from child to parent

questions.text: How do you stop event propagation?

exercises.title: Async Fetch Simulation
exercises.description: Create a function that returns a Promise. Resolve it with 'Success' after 2 seconds using setTimeout.

pageContent.description: The Document Object Model (DOM) is a programming interface for web documents. It represents the structure of a document as a tree of objects, allowing developers to interact with and manipulate the content, structure, and style of a web page. This chapter explores the fundamentals of the DOM and its usage in web development.
pageContent.para1: The DOM is a hierarchical representation of the HTML document, where each element, attribute, and piece of text is a node in the tree. By manipulating the DOM, developers can dynamically update the content and appearance of a web page.
pageContent.para3: Various methods can be used to select DOM elements, such as getElementById, getElementsByClassName, and querySelector. These methods return references to DOM nodes that can be manipulated.
pageContent.para4: Once an element is selected, its properties and content can be modified. This includes changing text content, attributes, and even the structure of the DOM.
pageContent.para5: New elements can be created and added to the DOM dynamically. This is useful for building dynamic interfaces and handling user interactions.

questions.text: The DOM represents the HTML document as what?
Which object represents the root of the DOM?
Is the DOM part of the core JS language?
Can JS modify CSS through the DOM?

exercises.title: Click Toggle
exercises.description: Write code to add a click listener to a button with ID 'btn'. When clicked, toggle a class 'active' on it.

pageContent.description: Manipulating the Document Object Model (DOM) using JavaScript is a fundamental skill for web developers. This chapter focuses on practical techniques and methods to dynamically modify, update, and interact with the DOM, providing a hands-on guide to enhance web page functionality.
pageContent.para1: DOM manipulation involves changing the content, structure, or style of a web page after it has been loaded. JavaScript provides a range of methods and properties to interact with the DOM dynamically.
pageContent.para3: Attributes of HTML elements, such as src, href, and class, can be modified dynamically using JavaScript. This is useful for updating the appearance and behavior of elements.
pageContent.para4: New elements can be added to the DOM, and existing ones can be removed. This allows for dynamic content creation and manipulation based on user interactions or other events.
pageContent.para5: Handling user interactions and events is a crucial aspect of DOM manipulation. Event listeners can be used to respond to clicks, keypresses, and other actions.

quizzes.title: Quiz: Manipulating the DOM with JS

questions.text: Which method selects an element by its ID?
How do you change the text content of an element?
Which method creates a new HTML element?
How do you add a child element to a parent?
How do you select all elements with a class?

exercises.title: Root Background
exercises.description: Find an element with ID 'root' and change its style.backgroundColor to 'lightblue'.

pageContent.description: Asynchronous JavaScript and XML (AJAX) and the Fetch API are essential tools for making asynchronous requests in web development. This chapter explores the concepts of AJAX, its evolution with the Fetch API, and how these technologies enable communication with servers, fetching data, and updating web pages without requiring a full page reload.
pageContent.para1: AJAX allows for the asynchronous exchange of data between the web browser and the server without requiring a full page reload. It enables the development of dynamic and interactive web applications by fetching and sending data in the background.
pageContent.para3: The Fetch API provides a modern and more powerful way to make asynchronous requests. It returns Promises and simplifies the process of handling responses.
pageContent.para4: Fetching JSON data is a common use case. The Fetch API makes it easy to handle JSON responses and extract the data for further use.
pageContent.para5: The Fetch API simplifies error handling by allowing you to use the catch method to handle network errors or failed HTTP requests.

questions.text: Which modern API is preferred over XMLHttpRequest?
How do you convert a fetch response to JSON?
What is the default HTTP method for fetch()?

exercises.title: Dynamic List Builder
exercises.description: Create a <ul> element using document.createElement and append 3 <li> items to it with text 'Task 1', 'Task 2', 'Task 3'.

pageContent.description: JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) is a lightweight data interchange format that is easy for humans to read and write. This chapter explores the fundamentals of JSON, its syntax, and how it is used for data serialization and exchange in web development.
pageContent.para1: JSON is a text-based data format that consists of key-value pairs, making it easy to represent structured data. It is commonly used for configuration files, data exchange between a server and a web application, and more.
pageContent.para3: JSON syntax is simple and consists of key-value pairs. Keys and string values are enclosed in double quotes, and values can be strings, numbers, objects, arrays, booleans, or null.
pageContent.para4: To work with JSON in JavaScript, you can parse JSON strings into JavaScript objects using the JSON.parse method. This is useful when handling data received from external sources.
pageContent.para5: JSON is commonly used in HTTP requests and responses. When sending data to a server or receiving data from it, JSON provides a standardized format for structured information.

questions.text: Which method converts a JS object to JSON string?
Which method converts a JSON string to JS object?
Are keys in JSON required to be in double quotes?

exercises.title: API Data Logger
exercises.description: Use the Fetch API to get data from 'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1' and log the title of the post.

pageContent.description: ECMAScript 6 (ES6) and subsequent versions bring significant enhancements and new features to JavaScript, improving its readability, expressiveness, and functionality. This chapter explores some of the key features introduced in ES6 and later versions, empowering developers with powerful tools for modern web development.
pageContent.para1: ES6, officially known as ECMAScript 2015, introduced several new features and enhancements to JavaScript. Subsequent versions, often referred to as ES6+, continued to add valuable features, making JavaScript a more versatile and expressive language.
pageContent.code1:
// Example of an ES6+ feature: Arrow Functions
const addNumbers = (a, b) => a + b;
pageContent.para3: Arrow functions provide a concise syntax for writing anonymous functions. They have a more compact syntax compared to traditional function expressions.
pageContent.code3:
// Example of Arrow Functions
const multiply = (x, y) => x * y;
pageContent.para4: Destructuring assignment allows you to extract values from arrays or objects and assign them to variables in a concise and expressive manner.
pageContent.para5: Template literals provide a more flexible way to create strings, allowing the embedding of expressions and multiline strings.

questions.text: Which feature allows embedding variables in strings?
What is the syntax for an Arrow Function?
What is the Spread operator symbol?
Which feature provides cleaner syntax for objects?

exercises.title: Object Serialization
exercises.description: Create a user object, convert it to a JSON string, then parse it back into a new object and log the name.

pageContent.description: Modularization is a crucial concept in modern JavaScript development, enabling the organization of code into reusable and manageable units. This chapter explores the use of modules in JavaScript and the role of module bundlers in optimizing and packaging code for web applications.
pageContent.para1: Modules are a way to organize and structure JavaScript code by encapsulating related functionality into separate files. They promote code reuse, maintainability, and help avoid naming conflicts.
pageContent.para3: Module bundlers optimize the loading of modules in web applications by combining them into a single file. This reduces the number of HTTP requests and enhances performance.
pageContent.para4: CommonJS and ES6 Modules are two popular module systems in JavaScript. While CommonJS is used in Node.js, ES6 Modules are widely adopted in browser-based development.
pageContent.para5: Webpack, Rollup, and Parcel are popular module bundlers that automate the process of bundling, optimizing, and managing dependencies in modern web applications.

questions.text: Which keyword is used to share code between files?
Which keyword is used to bring shared code in?
What is a common module bundler?
What is the benefit of using modules?
Can you have multiple default exports in a file?

exercises.title: Array Destructuring
exercises.description: Given an array ['red', 'green', 'blue'], use ES6 destructuring to assign the first two values to variables r and g.

pageContent.description: Promises and the Async/Await syntax are powerful tools for handling asynchronous operations in JavaScript. This chapter explores these features, providing a comprehensive guide on working with promises and simplifying asynchronous code using Async/Await.
pageContent.para1: Promises are objects that represent the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation. They provide a clean and structured way to handle asynchronous code, making it more readable and maintainable.
pageContent.para3: Async functions are functions that implicitly return a promise. They provide a cleaner syntax for handling asynchronous code compared to using plain promises.
pageContent.para4: The Await keyword can be used inside an async function to pause its execution until the promise is resolved. This simplifies the syntax for working with asynchronous code.
pageContent.para5: Error handling in Async/Await is done using try-catch blocks, providing a more synchronous-style approach to dealing with errors in asynchronous code.

questions.text: What does the await keyword do?
In which functions can you use await?
How do you handle errors in async/await?
Does an async function always return a Promise?

exercises.title: Custom Error
exercises.description: Write a function that throws an error 'Invalid Input' if a number is less than 0. Handle it with a catch block.

pageContent.description: Frontend frameworks are powerful tools that streamline the process of building dynamic and responsive user interfaces. These frameworks provide pre-built components, a structured architecture, and efficient development workflows, enabling developers to create modern and visually appealing web applications.
pageContent.para1: Frontend frameworks offer several advantages, including code reusability, maintainability, and a consistent structure. They often come with built-in features like state management, routing, and responsive design, saving developers time and effort.
pageContent.title41: React is a declarative, efficient, and flexible JavaScript library for building user interfaces. It allows developers to compose complex UIs from small and isolated pieces of code called 'components.'
pageContent.title51: Vue.js is a progressive JavaScript framework for building user interfaces. It is designed from the ground up to be incrementally adoptable, making it easy to integrate into existing projects.
pageContent.title61: Angular is a TypeScript-based open-source framework for building web applications. It offers a full-featured and highly opinionated solution, with a focus on modularity and maintainability.

questions.text: Main purpose of a frontend framework?
Which is NOT a frontend framework?
Why use frameworks instead of vanilla JS?

pageContent.description: React is a popular JavaScript library for building user interfaces, known for its component-based architecture and efficient rendering. This chapter introduces the fundamental concepts of React, providing a solid foundation for developing dynamic and interactive web applications.
pageContent.para1: React is a declarative, efficient, and flexible JavaScript library for building user interfaces. It allows developers to create reusable UI components and efficiently update the DOM by using a virtual DOM for optimal performance.
pageContent.title41: React applications are built using components, which are self-contained, reusable pieces of UI. Props are used to pass data from a parent component to its children.
pageContent.title51: State allows components to manage and update their internal data. The lifecycle methods provide hooks to perform actions at different stages of a component's life.
pageContent.title61: React provides a simple and consistent way to handle events within components. Event handlers are defined as methods and attached to specific events.

questions.text: How do you render a React app?

pageContent.description: React Router is a standard library for routing in React applications, enabling navigation between different components or pages. This chapter explores the usage of React Router to create dynamic and navigable user interfaces in React-based web applications.
pageContent.para1: React Router is a powerful navigation library for React applications, providing a declarative way to handle routing and navigation. It allows developers to define the structure of the application based on components and routes.
pageContent.title41: The BrowserRouter component provides the context for the router to work. It should be placed at the root of the component tree.
pageContent.title51: The Route component defines a route and the corresponding component that should be rendered when the path matches the current URL.
pageContent.title61: The Link component is used to create navigation links that allow users to navigate between different pages or components without triggering a full page reload.

questions.text: Which component is used for links?
What is a Single Page Application?
options: App that only works on mobile

questions.text: Which matches a URL path to component?

pageContent.description: State management is a critical aspect of building robust React applications. This chapter explores various state management techniques in React, including local component state, lifting state up, and using state management libraries like Redux, providing developers with tools to efficiently manage and share state across components.
pageContent.para1: In React, state represents the data that a component can maintain and modify. Proper state management is crucial for building dynamic and interactive user interfaces.
pageContent.title41: Local state is managed within a component using the `useState` hook. It is suitable for managing component-specific data.
pageContent.result: [State lifted up to the parent component]
pageContent.title51: Lifting state up involves managing state in a common ancestor component and passing it down to child components as props. This is useful for sharing state among components.
pageContent.title61: State management libraries like Redux provide a centralized store to manage application state. Actions and reducers are used to update and retrieve state.

questions.text: Which Hook is used for local state?
Name a tool for global state management.

pageContent.description: Styling is a crucial aspect of creating visually appealing and user-friendly React applications. This chapter explores various approaches to styling React components, including inline styles, CSS-in-JS libraries, and preprocessor integration, providing developers with options to suit their preferences and project requirements.
pageContent.para1: Styling in React involves applying visual styles to components to enhance the user interface. There are multiple approaches to styling, each with its own advantages and use cases.
pageContent.title41: Inline styles involve applying styles directly within the component using JavaScript objects. This approach allows for dynamic styling based on component state or props.
pageContent.title51: CSS-in-JS libraries like Styled-components or Emotion allow writing CSS directly in JavaScript files, providing a more component-centric styling approach with features like theming and dynamic styling.
pageContent.title61: Integrating preprocessor languages like Sass or Less with React allows developers to write more maintainable and structured styles with features like variables, mixins, and nested styles.

options: Pass JS object to style attr
questions.correct_answer: Pass JS object to style attr

pageContent.description: Forms play a crucial role in collecting user input in React applications. This chapter explores the various techniques for handling forms and user input, including controlled components, form validation, and the submission process, providing developers with the tools to create interactive and user-friendly forms.
pageContent.title1: Introduction to Handling Forms in React
pageContent.para1: Forms in React allow users to input data and interact with the application. Effectively handling forms involves managing the form state, controlling input elements, and validating user input.
pageContent.title41: Controlled components in React are forms where the form data is handled by the state of a React component. Input elements are controlled by React, allowing for dynamic updates and validation.
pageContent.title51: Form validation ensures that user input meets specific criteria before allowing form submission. In React, validation can be performed within controlled components, updating the form's valid state.
pageContent.title61: Handling form submission in React involves preventing the default form submission behavior, processing the form data, and triggering actions such as sending the data to a server.

quizzes.title: Quiz: Handling Forms and User Input

questions.text: Why use state for form inputs?
options: Keep UI and data in sync
questions.correct_answer: Keep UI and data in sync

pageContent.description: Understanding the lifecycle of a React component is essential for effective component management and optimization. This chapter explores the various lifecycle methods in React, including mounting, updating, and unmounting phases, providing insights into when and how to use each method to achieve specific behaviors and optimizations.
pageContent.para1: The lifecycle of a React component consists of various phases, each with associated methods. These methods allow developers to perform specific tasks at different stages of a component's existence, from creation to removal.
pageContent.title41: The mounting phase occurs when a component is being created and inserted into the DOM. Key lifecycle methods in this phase include constructor, render, componentDidMount.
pageContent.result: [Console logs: Constructor called, Render method called, Component did mount]
pageContent.title51: The updating phase occurs when a component is being re-rendered as a result of changes in props or state. Key lifecycle methods in this phase include render, componentDidUpdate.
pageContent.result1: [Console logs: Render method called, Component did update]
pageContent.title61: The unmounting phase occurs when a component is being removed from the DOM. The key lifecycle method in this phase is componentWillUnmount.
pageContent.result2: [Console logs: Render method called, Component will unmount]

pageContent.description: Angular and Vue are popular JavaScript frameworks for building modern, scalable, and maintainable web applications. This chapter provides an overview of both frameworks, highlighting their key features, architecture, and usage scenarios, helping developers make informed decisions based on their project requirements and preferences.
pageContent.para1: Angular is a comprehensive and opinionated JavaScript framework developed and maintained by Google. It follows the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architecture and provides a full-fledged ecosystem for building dynamic and feature-rich web applications.
pageContent.title41: Angular's two-way data binding simplifies the synchronization between the model and the view, ensuring that changes in one are automatically reflected in the other and vice versa.
pageContent.title51: Angular uses dependency injection to manage the creation and distribution of application components and services. This promotes modular and testable code.
pageContent.title61: Angular encourages the development of modular applications by organizing components, services, and other features into modules. This promotes code reusability and maintainability.

quizzes.title: Quiz: Introduction to Angular or Vue

options: Marker telling library what to do
questions.correct_answer: Marker telling library what to do

pageContent.description: Single Page Applications (SPAs) have become a popular approach for building responsive and seamless web experiences. This chapter provides an in-depth exploration of SPAs, covering their architecture, advantages, and key considerations in development, empowering developers to create modern and efficient web applications.
pageContent.para1: Single Page Applications (SPAs) are web applications that load a single HTML page and dynamically update the content as the user interacts with the application. SPAs provide a smooth and responsive user experience by eliminating page reloads.
pageContent.code1: // Example of a simple Single Page Application structure
pageContent.title41: SPAs load content dynamically, updating the DOM in response to user interactions without requiring full page reloads. This results in a smoother and more responsive user experience.
pageContent.title51: Client-side routing in SPAs allows navigation between different views or components without triggering a server request. This is achieved by updating the browser's URL and rendering the corresponding content.
pageContent.result1: [Client-side routing in a SPA using React Router]
pageContent.title61: SPAs often leverage asynchronous data loading to fetch data from a server without reloading the entire page. This is commonly achieved using AJAX, Fetch API, or other similar technologies.

questions.text: SPA fetch new HTML for every route?
Browser API for URL updates in SPA?

pageContent.description: Testing frontend code is a crucial aspect of ensuring the reliability and quality of web applications. This chapter delves into various testing strategies, tools, and best practices for frontend development, equipping developers with the skills to create robust and bug-free user interfaces.
pageContent.para1: Testing frontend code involves evaluating the behavior and performance of user interfaces to identify and fix bugs. This process helps maintain code quality, enhance user experience, and prevent regressions.
pageContent.code1: // Example of a simple frontend testing setup
pageContent.title41: Unit testing involves testing individual components or functions in isolation to ensure they perform as expected. This approach aids in early bug detection and promotes modular code development.
pageContent.title51: Integration testing verifies the interaction and collaboration between different components to ensure they work together seamlessly. This type of testing helps identify issues that may arise when combining multiple modules.
pageContent.title61: End-to-End testing evaluates the entire application flow from start to finish. It ensures that all components, including the frontend and backend, work together harmoniously to deliver the intended user experience.

pageContent.description: Node.js is a powerful JavaScript runtime built on the V8 JavaScript engine. It allows developers to execute JavaScript code on the server-side, enabling the development of scalable and high-performance network applications. Node.js is known for its event-driven, non-blocking I/O model, making it well-suited for handling concurrent requests.
pageContent.para1: Node.js comes with several key features that make it a popular choice for building server-side applications. These features include a single-threaded event loop, asynchronous and non-blocking operations, and a vast ecosystem of packages available through npm (Node Package Manager).
pageContent.title41: Node.js uses the CommonJS module system to organize code into reusable and maintainable modules. Each file in Node.js is treated as a separate module, and modules can be loaded using the 'require' function.
pageContent.title51: npm is the default package manager for Node.js, providing a vast ecosystem of open-source packages. Developers can easily install, manage, and share packages using npm.
pageContent.title61: In addition to built-in modules, Node.js allows developers to create and use custom modules, enabling code organization and reusability.

pageContent.description: Understanding Node.js modules and utilizing the Node Package Manager (NPM) are fundamental skills for Node.js developers. This chapter explores the concept of modules, their role in Node.js applications, and how NPM streamlines the process of managing dependencies and packages.
pageContent.para1: Node.js modules provide a modular and scalable approach to organize and structure code in Node.js applications. Modules enable code reuse, maintainability, and encapsulation of functionality.
pageContent.title41: Creating Node.js modules involves encapsulating code within a file and defining the module's functionalities. This modular approach enhances code organization and maintainability.
pageContent.title51: Node.js modules use export and import mechanisms to share functionality between files. This allows developers to use and reuse modules in different parts of their applications.
pageContent.title61: Node.js supports both CommonJS modules and ES modules. Understanding the differences and choosing the appropriate module system is essential for compatibility and code interoperability.

pageContent.description: Building RESTful APIs using Express.js is a core skill for web developers. This chapter explores the powerful Express.js framework and guides developers through creating scalable, efficient, and well-structured APIs for their web applications.
pageContent.para1: RESTful APIs provide a standardized way for different applications to communicate over the web. Express.js simplifies the process of building and handling RESTful APIs by providing a robust framework for Node.js.
pageContent.title3: Key Concepts of RESTful APIs with Express.js
pageContent.title41: Express.js simplifies API routing by allowing developers to define routes for different HTTP methods. This enables the creation of well-organized and structured APIs with clear endpoint definitions.
pageContent.title51: Middleware functions in Express.js allow developers to execute code at various stages of the request-response cycle. This flexibility enables tasks such as authentication, logging, and error handling.
pageContent.title61: Express.js simplifies request and response handling, making it easy to send and receive data in various formats. JSON is commonly used, allowing seamless integration with frontend applications.
pageContent.result2: [Handling JSON data in an Express.js application]

pageContent.description: Interacting with databases is a fundamental aspect of many web applications. This chapter explores techniques for connecting, querying, and managing databases, providing developers with the knowledge to build robust and data-driven web applications.
pageContent.para1: Database interaction involves connecting web applications to databases for storing, retrieving, and managing data. Understanding database concepts and employing effective interaction methods are crucial for building scalable and efficient applications.
pageContent.title41: CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations are fundamental in database interaction. This chapter covers how to perform these operations using various database systems and frameworks.
pageContent.result: [Performing CRUD operations with MongoDB and Mongoose]
pageContent.title51: ORM simplifies database interaction by mapping database entities to objects in code. This abstraction streamlines data manipulation and allows developers to work with databases using familiar programming paradigms.
pageContent.result1: [Using Sequelize ORM in a Node.js application]
pageContent.title61: Connection pooling optimizes database interaction by managing and reusing database connections. This helps improve application performance and resource utilization, especially in scenarios with frequent database queries.
pageContent.result2: [Using connection pooling in a Node.js application]

pageContent.description: Implementing secure authentication and authorization mechanisms is crucial for safeguarding web applications. This chapter explores best practices, techniques, and frameworks for user authentication and authorization, ensuring the protection of sensitive resources.
pageContent.para1: Authentication verifies the identity of users, while authorization determines the actions users are allowed to perform. Proper implementation of these processes is essential for protecting user data and maintaining the integrity of web applications.
pageContent.title3: Key Concepts of Authentication and Authorization
pageContent.title41: User registration and login processes are foundational for authentication. This chapter covers how to securely handle user registration, store user credentials, and authenticate users during login.
pageContent.result: [User registration and login with Express.js and bcrypt]
pageContent.title51: Token-based authentication enhances security by generating and validating tokens for user sessions. This chapter demonstrates how to implement token-based authentication for secure communication between clients and servers.
pageContent.title61: Role-based access control defines permissions based on user roles. This chapter explores how to implement role-based access control to restrict or grant access to specific resources based on user roles.
pageContent.result2: [Implementing role-based access control in an Express.js application]

pageContent.description: Middleware functions play a crucial role in the Express.js framework, enabling developers to customize the request-response cycle. This chapter explores the concept of middleware, its implementation, and how it enhances the functionality and flexibility of Express.js applications.
pageContent.para1: Middleware functions in Express.js are functions that have access to the request, response, and the next middleware function in the application's request-response cycle. They can perform tasks such as logging, authentication, data transformation, and error handling.
pageContent.title3: Key Concepts of Middleware in Express.js
pageContent.title41: Application-level middleware is bound to an instance of the Express.js application. It executes for every incoming request, providing a centralized location for tasks such as logging, authentication, and setting headers.
pageContent.title51: Router-level middleware is specific to a particular route or group of routes defined by an instance of `express.Router()`. It allows developers to apply middleware only to a subset of routes within the application.
pageContent.title61: Error-handling middleware functions receive errors as parameters and can be used to centralize error handling in an Express.js application. They are typically defined as the last middleware in the stack.

pageContent.description: Proper error handling is crucial for building robust and resilient Node.js applications. This chapter explores best practices, strategies, and tools for handling errors effectively, ensuring a stable and reliable application even in unexpected scenarios.
pageContent.title1: Introduction to Error Handling in Node.js
pageContent.para1: Error handling in Node.js involves identifying, logging, and gracefully recovering from errors that may occur during the execution of a program. Adopting effective error handling practices enhances the reliability and maintainability of Node.js applications.
pageContent.title3: Key Concepts of Error Handling in Node.js
pageContent.title41: Global error handling is a strategy that captures unhandled errors at the application level. It provides a centralized location to handle unexpected errors, preventing them from crashing the entire application.
pageContent.result: [Global error handling in a Node.js application]
pageContent.title51: Handling promise rejections is essential in Node.js applications utilizing promises. This chapter covers techniques to handle promise rejections and prevent unhandled promise rejections from terminating the application.
pageContent.result1: [Handling promise rejections in a Node.js application]
pageContent.title61: Logging and monitoring are crucial components of error handling, providing insights into the application's behavior and performance. This chapter explores the integration of logging and monitoring tools for effective error tracking.
pageContent.result2: [Integrating a logging library in a Node.js application]

pageContent.description: Real-time communication is a vital aspect of modern web applications, enabling instant updates and interactions. This chapter explores techniques, protocols, and frameworks for implementing real-time communication features, enhancing the responsiveness and user experience of web applications.
pageContent.para1: Real-time communication allows instant data transfer and updates between clients and servers, providing a dynamic and interactive user experience. This chapter delves into the concepts and technologies that make real-time communication possible in web applications.
pageContent.title41: WebSocket is a communication protocol that enables bidirectional, real-time communication between clients and servers. This chapter covers the basics of WebSocket and how it facilitates low-latency communication for web applications.
pageContent.title51: Socket.io is a popular JavaScript library that simplifies real-time communication in web applications. This chapter explores the features of Socket.io and how it abstracts the complexity of handling WebSocket connections.
pageContent.result1: [Using Socket.io for real-time communication in an Express.js application]
pageContent.title61: Real-time communication follows an event-driven architecture, where events trigger actions or updates. This chapter explores the principles of event-driven design and how it enhances the responsiveness of web applications.

pageContent.description: Testing is a critical aspect of backend development, ensuring the reliability and correctness of server-side code. This chapter explores various testing methodologies, tools, and best practices for testing backend code in Node.js applications.
pageContent.para1: Testing backend code involves verifying that server-side functionalities work as expected. This chapter introduces the importance of testing, different testing types, and how testing contributes to the overall quality and stability of backend applications.
pageContent.title3: Key Concepts of Testing Backend Code
pageContent.title41: Unit testing involves testing individual units or components of backend code in isolation. This chapter covers the principles of unit testing and demonstrates how to write and execute unit tests using popular testing frameworks.
pageContent.result: [Unit test suite using Mocha and Chai]
pageContent.title51: Integration testing verifies the interactions between different components of a backend system. This chapter explores how to design and conduct integration tests to ensure the seamless collaboration of server-side modules.
pageContent.title61: End-to-end testing evaluates the entire application's functionality, simulating user interactions and scenarios. This chapter guides developers through the process of designing and executing end-to-end tests for Node.js applications.

questions.text: Name a test runner for Node?

pageContent.description: Deploying and hosting web applications is a crucial step in making them accessible to users. This chapter explores various deployment strategies, hosting platforms, and best practices to ensure a smooth and reliable deployment process for your web applications.
pageContent.para1: Deployment and hosting involve making a web application available to users on the internet. This chapter introduces the importance of deployment, different hosting options, and considerations for choosing the right deployment strategy for your application.
pageContent.title3: Key Concepts of Deployment and Hosting
pageContent.title41: PaaS providers offer platforms that abstract the underlying infrastructure, simplifying deployment and management. This chapter explores how to deploy applications to PaaS platforms like Heroku, reducing the operational overhead for developers.
pageContent.result: [Deploying a Dockerized application to Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)]
pageContent.title51: IaaS providers offer virtualized computing resources on-demand. This chapter explores deploying applications to IaaS platforms like AWS EC2, providing more control over the infrastructure configuration.
pageContent.result1: [Deploying a Node.js application to AWS EC2]
pageContent.title61: Containerization with tools like Docker and orchestration with Kubernetes provide a scalable and portable deployment solution. This chapter explores containerizing applications, managing containers, and orchestrating deployments for increased efficiency.
pageContent.result2: [Creating a Docker container and deploying with Kubernetes]

pageContent.description: Integrating frontend and backend systems is a crucial aspect of web development, enabling the creation of dynamic and interactive web applications. This process involves establishing communication between the user interface (frontend) and server-side logic (backend), allowing seamless data exchange and user interaction.
pageContent.para1: Various communication methods exist for integrating frontend and backend components. Common approaches include AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML) for making asynchronous requests, RESTful APIs (Representational State Transfer) for structured data exchange, and WebSockets for real-time bidirectional communication.
pageContent.title41: In RESTful API design, endpoints represent specific resources. A well-designed API defines meaningful and intuitive endpoints for different functionalities.
pageContent.title51: HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) are used to perform different operations on resources. Proper usage of HTTP methods ensures adherence to RESTful principles.
pageContent.title61: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a common data format for RESTful APIs. It provides a lightweight and human-readable way to structure and exchange data between frontend and backend.

pageContent.description: Implementing secure authentication is paramount for protecting user accounts and sensitive information. This chapter explores authentication mechanisms, best practices, and frameworks for building robust authentication systems in web applications.
pageContent.para1: Authentication is the process of verifying the identity of users and ensuring they have the necessary permissions to access specific resources. This chapter introduces the importance of authentication, common authentication methods, and the role of secure practices in safeguarding user accounts.
pageContent.title41: User registration and login processes are foundational for authentication. This chapter covers how to securely handle user registration, store user credentials, and authenticate users during login.
pageContent.result: [User registration and login with Express.js and bcrypt]
pageContent.title51: Token-based authentication enhances security by generating and validating tokens for user sessions. This chapter demonstrates how to implement token-based authentication for secure communication between clients and servers.
pageContent.title61: OAuth and OpenID Connect are industry-standard protocols for authentication and authorization. This chapter explores how to integrate OAuth and OpenID Connect into your application to enable secure third-party authentication.

pageContent.description: Efficient state management is crucial for building scalable and responsive web applications. This chapter explores various state management techniques, libraries, and best practices to effectively manage the state of your application.
pageContent.para1: State management involves maintaining and synchronizing the state of your application across different components. This chapter introduces the importance of state management, common challenges, and the role of effective state management in creating a seamless user experience.
pageContent.title41: Local component state is specific to a particular component and is managed within that component. This chapter covers the basics of managing local state in React components and demonstrates how it provides encapsulation and simplicity.
pageContent.result: [Local state management in a React component]
pageContent.title51: The Context API in React allows for the sharing of state between components without passing props manually. This chapter explores how to use the Context API to manage global state and avoid prop drilling.
pageContent.result1: [Using Context API for global state management in React]
pageContent.title61: Several libraries, such as Redux and MobX, provide solutions for global state management in larger applications. This chapter explores the principles and usage of these libraries to handle complex state requirements.
pageContent.result2: [Using Redux for global state management in a React application]

pageContent.description: Achieving optimal performance is crucial for delivering a seamless user experience in web applications. This chapter explores performance optimization techniques, tools, and best practices to ensure your application runs efficiently and responsively.
pageContent.para1: Performance optimization involves enhancing the speed and responsiveness of web applications. This chapter introduces the significance of performance optimization, common challenges, and the impact of a well-optimized application on user satisfaction.
pageContent.title41: Minification reduces the size of source code by removing unnecessary characters, while compression further reduces file sizes during transmission. This chapter explores how minifying and compressing assets contribute to faster loading times.
pageContent.result: [Using a build tool for minification and compression]
pageContent.title51: Lazy loading delays the loading of non-essential resources until they are needed, reducing initial page load times. This chapter demonstrates how to implement lazy loading for images, scripts, and other assets.
pageContent.title61: Caching involves storing copies of files to reduce server requests and improve load times. This chapter explores various caching strategies, including browser caching, CDN caching, and server-side caching.
pageContent.result2: [Setting cache headers for static assets in an Express.js application]

pageContent.description: Ensuring the security of your web applications is paramount to protect against vulnerabilities and unauthorized access. This chapter explores security best practices, common threats, and strategies to safeguard your applications from potential risks.
pageContent.para1: Security best practices are essential for safeguarding web applications against various threats and vulnerabilities. This chapter introduces the importance of security, common risks, and the role of implementing best practices in building secure and robust applications.
pageContent.title3: Key Concepts of Security Best Practices
pageContent.title41: Securely handling user passwords is fundamental to application security. This chapter covers the best practices for storing and managing passwords, including the use of secure hashing algorithms and salting.
pageContent.title51: Input validation and sanitization prevent malicious input and help mitigate common security threats, such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting. This chapter demonstrates how to implement robust input validation and sanitization techniques.
pageContent.result1: [Input validation and sanitization in an Express.js application]
pageContent.title61: XSS attacks involve injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. This chapter explores strategies to prevent XSS attacks, including input validation, output encoding, and Content Security Policy (CSP).

pageContent.description: Smooth integration and deployment processes are crucial for delivering and maintaining successful web applications. This chapter explores integration strategies, continuous integration, and deployment pipelines to ensure a streamlined and efficient workflow.
pageContent.para1: Integration and deployment are essential stages in the lifecycle of a web application. This chapter introduces the significance of seamless integration, continuous integration practices, and strategies for deploying applications to various environments.
pageContent.code1:
// Example of setting up a basic continuous integration (CI) pipeline
// Using a CI/CD service like GitHub Actions
name: CI
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v2
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm install
      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test
pageContent.title3: Key Concepts of Integration and Deployment
pageContent.title41: Version control systems, such as Git, play a crucial role in managing and tracking changes in your codebase. This chapter covers best practices for version control integration, collaborative development, and branching strategies.
pageContent.title51: Continuous Integration ensures that changes made to the codebase are automatically tested and validated. This chapter explores setting up CI pipelines, running automated tests, and integrating CI services like GitHub Actions or Jenkins.
pageContent.code3:
// Example of running automated tests in a CI pipeline
// (Assuming a testing framework is set up in the project)
// CI configuration file (e.g., .github/workflows/ci.yml)
name: CI
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v2
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm install
      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test
pageContent.result1: [Running automated tests in a CI pipeline]
pageContent.title61: Choosing the right deployment strategy is crucial for delivering updates and new features to users. This chapter explores various deployment strategies, including blue-green deployment, canary releases, and feature toggles.

pageContent.description: Scalability is essential for handling growing user loads and ensuring optimal performance in web applications. This chapter explores strategies, architectures, and best practices for building scalable applications that can efficiently adapt to increased demand.
pageContent.para1: Scalability is the ability of a web application to handle increased load without sacrificing performance. This chapter introduces the importance of scalability, common challenges, and the role of scalable architectures in building robust applications.
pageContent.title41: Horizontal scaling involves adding more instances of a service to distribute the load and increase capacity. This chapter covers the principles of horizontal scaling and its application in building scalable web applications.
pageContent.title51: Microservices break down a monolithic application into smaller, independently deployable services. This chapter explores the principles of microservices architecture, its benefits, and considerations for building and maintaining a microservices-based system.
pageContent.result1: [Defining a simple microservice in a Node.js application]
pageContent.title61: Load balancing evenly distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers or resources to prevent overloading a single instance. This chapter explores load balancing techniques, including DNS-based, hardware, and software load balancing.

pageContent.description: Microservices architecture is a modern approach to building scalable and maintainable applications by breaking them down into small, independent services. This chapter explores the principles, benefits, and challenges of microservices, providing insights into designing and implementing a microservices-based system.
pageContent.para1: Microservices architecture involves breaking down a monolithic application into small, independent services that communicate through well-defined APIs. This chapter introduces the fundamentals of microservices, highlighting its advantages and considerations in application development.
pageContent.title41: Microservices are designed to be independent entities, each responsible for a specific business capability. This chapter explores the concept of service independence, emphasizing how it allows teams to develop, deploy, and scale services autonomously.
pageContent.result: [Defining a microservice with a dedicated functionality]
pageContent.title51: In microservices architecture, each service manages its own data, reducing dependencies on a centralized database. This chapter delves into the principles of decentralized data management, including database per service and eventual consistency.
pageContent.result1: [Decentralized data management in a microservices system]
pageContent.title61: Microservices offer scalability by allowing individual services to be scaled independently based on demand. Additionally, fault isolation ensures that issues in one service do not affect the entire system. This chapter explores scalability and fault isolation strategies.

pageContent.description: GraphQL is a query language and runtime for APIs that enables clients to request only the data they need. This chapter provides a fundamental understanding of GraphQL, covering its syntax, schema definition, and the benefits it offers over traditional REST APIs.
pageContent.para1: GraphQL is a modern alternative to REST APIs, offering a more flexible and efficient way to interact with data. This chapter introduces the basics of GraphQL, highlighting its key features and the problems it aims to solve.
pageContent.title41: GraphQL allows clients to request exactly the data they need, reducing over-fetching and under-fetching of data. This chapter explores the concept of declarative data fetching in GraphQL queries.
pageContent.result: [Declarative data fetching in a GraphQL query]
pageContent.title51: Unlike REST APIs that often have multiple endpoints for different resources, GraphQL has a single endpoint for all data operations. This chapter discusses the benefits of a single GraphQL endpoint and how it simplifies the API structure.
pageContent.title61: GraphQL uses a strongly typed schema to define the structure of the API and the types of data it can return. This chapter explores the benefits of a strongly typed schema in GraphQL and how it enhances development.

options: Clients get exactly what they need
questions.correct_answer: Clients get exactly what they need

pageContent.description: This chapter delves into advanced concepts and features of GraphQL, along with the integration of the Apollo Client for efficient data management in client-side applications. Explore topics such as mutations, subscriptions, caching strategies, and optimizing GraphQL queries.
pageContent.para1: As developers become more proficient with GraphQL, it's essential to delve into advanced concepts that enhance the capabilities of the API. This chapter covers advanced GraphQL topics, including mutations, subscriptions, and optimizing queries for better performance.
pageContent.title41: GraphQL mutations allow clients to modify data on the server. This chapter explores the use of mutations for performing operations such as creating, updating, and deleting data.
pageContent.result: [GraphQL mutation for creating a new post]
pageContent.title51: GraphQL subscriptions enable real-time communication between the client and the server, allowing clients to receive updates when specific events occur. This chapter demonstrates the implementation of GraphQL subscriptions for real-time data.
pageContent.title61: Optimizing GraphQL queries is crucial for efficient data retrieval and improved performance. This chapter covers techniques such as batching and caching to optimize GraphQL queries on the client side.
pageContent.result2: [Using Apollo Client to cache and batch GraphQL queries]

quizzes.title: Quiz: Advanced GraphQL and Apollo Client

pageContent.description: Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) are a type of web application that utilizes modern web capabilities to deliver an app-like experience to users. PWAs are designed to be reliable, fast, and engaging, providing a seamless experience across various devices and network conditions. They leverage service workers, responsive design, and other technologies to offer features traditionally associated with native mobile applications.
pageContent.para1: PWAs exhibit key characteristics that contribute to their success, including responsiveness, offline functionality, and the ability to be installed on the user's device. These features enhance the user experience and bridge the gap between web and native applications.
pageContent.title41: Service workers enable offline caching, allowing PWAs to function even in the absence of a network connection. Cached resources are retrieved locally, providing a reliable user experience.
pageContent.title51: Background sync allows PWAs to synchronize data in the background, ensuring that updates are sent or received even if the app is not actively in use. This feature enhances real-time functionality.
pageContent.title61: PWAs can leverage push notifications to engage users and provide timely updates. Push notifications are sent even when the PWA is not open, enhancing user re-engagement.

options: Web app feeling like native mobile
questions.correct_answer: Web app feeling like native mobile

pageContent.description: This chapter explores the integration of WebAssembly with JavaScript to leverage the performance benefits of low-level languages while maintaining the flexibility of high-level languages. Learn about the basics of WebAssembly, its use cases, and how to work with it alongside JavaScript.
pageContent.para1: WebAssembly (Wasm) is a binary instruction format that enables high-performance execution on web browsers. This chapter introduces the basics of WebAssembly, its advantages, and how it complements JavaScript in web development.
pageContent.title41: WebAssembly allows developers to run code at near-native speed by providing a low-level instruction format. This chapter explores the performance advantages of WebAssembly and scenarios where low-level execution is crucial.
pageContent.result: [Using WebAssembly to perform a computationally intensive task in JavaScript]
pageContent.title51: WebAssembly seamlessly integrates with JavaScript, allowing developers to call WebAssembly functions from JavaScript and vice versa. This chapter demonstrates the integration of WebAssembly with JavaScript for enhanced functionality.
pageContent.code3:
// Example of calling a WebAssembly function from JavaScript
const result = wasmModule.exports.add(5, 7);

pageContent.description: Explore the paradigm of serverless architecture, where the focus shifts from managing servers to executing code in response to events. This chapter covers the fundamentals of serverless computing, its benefits, and how to leverage serverless platforms for scalable and cost-effective solutions.
pageContent.para1: Serverless architecture is a cloud computing paradigm that eliminates the need for traditional server management. This chapter provides an introduction to serverless computing, highlighting its key concepts and advantages in building modern applications.
pageContent.title41: Serverless functions are triggered by events, such as HTTP requests, database changes, or file uploads. This chapter explores the event-driven nature of serverless architecture and how it enables scalable and responsive applications.
pageContent.title51: Serverless platforms automatically scale resources based on demand, and users only pay for the actual execution time of functions. This chapter discusses the benefits of auto-scaling and the cost-effectiveness of the pay-per-use model in serverless computing.
pageContent.title61: Serverless functions are designed to be stateless, meaning they don't retain information between executions. This chapter explores the stateless nature of serverless functionality and its implications for application design.

options: Pay only for what you use
questions.correct_answer: Pay only for what you use

pageContent.description: Dive into the fascinating world of machine learning, where computers learn patterns from data to make predictions or decisions. This chapter introduces the fundamentals of machine learning, various algorithms, and how to implement machine learning models using popular frameworks.
pageContent.para1: Machine learning is a field of artificial intelligence that enables computers to learn from data and improve their performance over time. This chapter provides an introduction to the basics of machine learning, its types, and its applications in real-world scenarios.

pageContent.description: Discover the power of reusable and encapsulated UI components with Web Components. This chapter introduces the concept of Web Components, explains how to create custom elements, and demonstrates their usage for building modular and maintainable web applications.
pageContent.para1: Web Components are a set of web platform APIs that enable the creation of reusable and encapsulated custom elements. This chapter provides an introduction to Web Components, highlighting their role in building modular and maintainable web applications.
pageContent.title41: Web Components consist of custom elements and Shadow DOM, providing encapsulation and isolation for component styling and behavior. This chapter explores the creation of custom elements and the usage of Shadow DOM in Web Components.
